God confronts Jerusalem through Ezekiel with a metaphor so raw and painful that it demands we sit with the actual depth of what Israel has done. He begins not with accusation but with rescue. Jerusalem was abandoned at birth—left to die, unwanted, covered in blood. But God passed by and chose her. He washed away the filth of her existence, clothed her in splendor, adorned her with jewels, and made her His bride. She became beautiful not through her own merit but through His extravagant grace. This is not background information. This is the foundation upon which everything else rests. A reader who does not feel the weight of God's initiative—His inexplicable decision to save her when she deserved death—has missed what makes the next part devastatingly real.

And then she used everything He gave her to betray Him. The jewelry became idols. The fine garments draped idols. The food He provided was burned as offerings to demons. Her children—born to Him, belonging to Him—were offered to false gods. Most chilling is that she did not simply accept the gifts of lovers; she *paid* them. She corrupted the very nature of commerce and covenant. She was not a prostitute seeking survival but a wife who had become so twisted that she paid others to take what was never theirs to receive. The metaphor is obscene because the sin is obscene. She had been brought from death to queenship and chose to debase herself with the worship of demons and the murder of her own children in their names.

But there is something harder here still: Jerusalem had forgotten. Verse 22 cuts to the heart—"you did not remember the days of your youth when you were naked and bare, wallowing in your own blood." She had erased the memory of her rescue. She had rewritten her own story as though her beauty and power were her own achievements, not gifts. This forgetting was itself a kind of rebellion. Without remembrance of what she had been saved from, there was no gratitude, no covenant loyalty, no shame. God's judgment that follows—the exposure, the destruction, the violence—is not arbitrary cruelty. It is what happens when a covenant is shattered and a rescued people choose to unmake themselves.

Yet even in this chapter of divine wrath, something unexpected appears. God says His anger will one day be laid to rest. His jealousy will turn away. He will be calm. The chapter does not end in condemnation alone but gestures toward a future where even this broken covenant might be restored. The judgment is real and terrible, but it is not final. God's determination to save Jerusalem did not end when she sinned; it was tested. And that same God who passed by and said "Live" to a dying infant will not let her remain dead forever. We are meant to ask: what kind of God pursues such a people? And what does that tell us about how He pursues us?
